<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me plan what changes are needed for mobile responsiveness:

1. **NavBar** - Add hamburger menu for mobile (links hidden at 900px with no alternative)
2. **Hero** - Already has 768px breakpoint, but need to check height on mobile (min-height: 100vh should be fine but text may be too large)
3. **Services** - Add `id="services"` to the section, and show desc on mobile in a better way
4. **Projects** - The sticky stacking with fixed `stickyTop` values from `content.js` won't work well on mobile. 

I need to disable the sticky positioning on mobile devices. The **Process** section's watermark numbers might be oversized, and padding needs adjustment across the board. **About** section's character animation with large typography could overflow on narrow screens. The **CustomCursor** should already handle touch devices correctly through the fine pointer check. I should also verify that section padding isn't excessive and that the scatter animation offsets in Services won't cause overflow issues on smaller viewports.

For the specific fixes, I'm planning to add a hamburger menu toggle that appears at 900px and below, with a mobile drawer overlay. The **Hero** section's responsive title sizing should work fine on phones, though I should double-check if the 100vh minimum height feels too tall on mobile. For **Services**, I need to add an id to the section and monitor whether the scatter animation's large offsets cause clipping problems on mobile—though the overflow hidden should contain them properly.

On **Projects**, the sticky card stacking with those small top offsets will create heavy overlap on mobile, so I should disable the sticky positioning entirely on smaller screens and just show them as a regular list. For **Process**, the single-column grid works well on mobile, and the watermark sizing looks reasonable, though I need to verify the padding values don't create awkward spacing.

For **About**, the character animation font scales appropriately, but the section padding needs a mobile override to prevent excessive side padding. And for **Contact**, the top padding might be too generous on mobile and could benefit from adjustment.

Now I'm mapping out the specific fixes needed across the components. The navbar needs a hamburger menu, Services needs an anchor ID and better mobile description handling, Projects should disable sticky positioning on mobile, and several sections need padding adjustments at smaller breakpoints. I should also check if the Hero and Marquee sections need any mobile refinements.

Starting with the navbar - I'm adding state management for a mobile menu toggle.

Adding hamburger menu to NavBar... Finishing the navigation component with a call-to-action button and hamburger menu toggle, then moving into the CSS to handle the mobile responsive behavior with the menu appearing as a drawer on smaller screens.

Now I'm working through the remaining layout adjustments: adding the services section ID for anchor linking, disabling the sticky positioning on mobile devices, and reconsidering how the service descriptions display at different breakpoints. The current mobile layout at 700px and below hides the description and metrics to keep things clean, which seems like the right call for phone screens where the title alone provides enough context.

I'm also going through the padding overrides needed across several sections—the process area, stack section, about banner, and contact section all need adjustments for smaller screens. The hero buttons need to stack properly on very small devices too.

Looking at the FeaturedWork component, it has perspective transforms and a terminal-like interface that scales down nicely on mobile with reduced font sizes. The Marquee also scales from 27px to 20px on smaller screens.

For the Services scatter animation, I'm concerned about the scatter offsets being ±480px horizontally and ±900px vertically—on a 375px phone, these strips would scatter completely off-screen. Since the section has overflow hidden, they'd be clipped and invisible during the animation, which might look broken when they're supposed to assemble back together. The animation should bring them back into view once the scroll threshold is hit. I'm wondering if there's an issue with how the scroll detection works when `overflow: hidden` is applied—the `useScroll` hook targets the section with an offset range, so it should track correctly regardless. Since the clipping is intentional and the assembly trigger fires based on scroll progress, this should work fine on mobile too. Let me look at the description styling next.

I'm thinking about showing the description text on mobile for better UX, even though it makes the strips taller. I'll adjust the grid layout to accommodate the body content and update the breakpoint to display the description with appropriate sizing. Now I need to map out the specific file changes needed.

I'm going through each component to identify what needs updating: the navbar needs mobile menu functionality, Services needs an ID anchor, and several CSS files need mobile adjustments like disabling sticky positioning on smaller screens and reducing padding. I'm also checking whether the sticky top values for project items will cause layout issues on mobile devices. Adding mobile styles... The `about-char-line` uses `overflow: hidden` for the reveal animation, but this clips content that wraps to multiple lines. Since "I build AI that" is 15 characters and needs to fit on a single line at mobile sizes, I need to reduce the font size for smaller screens to prevent the text from wrapping and getting clipped. I'm working through the math on character widths for the condensed font—at 43px I'd get roughly 330px for the longest line, which fits the 335px content area on mobile. I'll adjust the clamp value to something like `clamp(28px, 8vw, 140px)` or add a specific mobile breakpoint to handle this more precisely. Adjusting the clamp value to `clamp(28px, 9vw, 140px)` should solve this—at 375px that gives 33.75px, which lets 15 characters fit within the 335px content area with room to spare. The sizing scales reasonably across larger viewports too.

Now I'm looking at the Services section on mobile. The description is hidden at the 700px breakpoint, but I should show it on smaller screens for better context. I'm also rethinking the NavBar mobile menu—instead of a simple hamburger dropdown, I want a full-screen slide-in menu that feels cleaner and more aligned with the minimal dark design.

Let me map out all the changes I need to make... Let me quickly review the Marquee CSS file too.</details></think>

Now I have all the information I need. Let me plan the changes:

1. **NavBar.jsx** - Add hamburger menu with state
2. **NavBar.css** - Add hamburger button styles and mobile overlay menu
3. **Services.jsx** - Add `id="services"` to section
4. **Services.css** - Show desc on mobile, reduce scatter concern
5. **Projects.css** - Disable sticky on mobile, add mobile padding/min-height fixes
6. **Process.css** - Fix padding on small screens
7. **About.css** - Fix `about-char` font clamp, fix `stack-section` padding


8. **Contact.css** - Reduce section top padding on mobile
9. **Hero.css** - Possibly reduce min-height on mobile, adjust padding

Now I'm implementing these changes. For the NavBar, I'll create a hamburger menu that appears at 900px and below, with a clean three-line icon that toggles a dropdown overlay containing the navigation links.

Writing NavBar component... Finishing the navigation component with the mobile menu overlay that toggles open and closed, mapping through the nav links and closing the menu when a link is clicked.

Still writing styles... Still writing styles... Still writing styles...

Now I'm thinking through the z-index layering for the mobile menu—the nav bar is at 9100, so I need to position the menu carefully so it doesn't hide the hamburger button. I'm considering placing the menu at 9050 to keep it below the nav while still appearing above other content.

For the services section on mobile, I need to adjust the layout at smaller breakpoints to show the description text instead of hiding it, and I'm restructuring the grid to be narrower with adjusted padding.

For projects, I'm disabling the sticky positioning on mobile and reducing the overall padding to save space. And for the process section, I'm adding horizontal padding overrides across different breakpoints to ensure proper spacing on smaller screens.

Now I'm adjusting the about section—fixing the character font size to scale responsively on very small screens, and then reconsidering how the stack section's padding interacts with its parent container since both have their own padding values defined.

Adding media queries to ensure the stack section's horizontal padding matches the section-inner's 20px constraint on tablets and below, and similarly tightening the contact section's padding for mobile.

For the hero, the viewport-based font sizing works well on phones—at 375px width, the title clamps to 58px which fits the three-line layout reasonably. I'll keep the 100vh minimum height since that's appropriate for a hero section on mobile.

Now I'm checking if the "I build AI that" text will actually fit at 46px with the condensed font weight. With the width setting at 72, characters are roughly half the font size, so 15 characters at ~23px each would need about 345px—but I only have 335px of available width after accounting for padding. This might be too tight.

I'm considering lowering the minimum to 30px instead, which would give me about 228px of width needed—plenty of breathing room. But I need to think through how this affects the responsive scaling across different screen sizes, since changing the clamp value shifts where the breakpoints occur. (30, 54) = 54px, so they match at that breakpoint. Going down to 500px, the original stays at 46px while the new value drops to 45px, and it gets progressively smaller at 400px and 375px, which is exactly what we need to prevent the overflow. So switching to `clamp(30px, 9vw, 140px)` should fix the issue without breaking anything else.

I'm also checking the `about-grid` component—it switches to a single column at 900px, which means tablets at 768px will already be single column, and the `section-inner` padding handles the spacing. Now I need to look at the `about-word-line` component and its perspective and font-size settings.

For the Services section, I'm reconsidering whether to show the description text on mobile. The descriptions are fairly long, so they'll add vertical height, but that's acceptable since mobile users expect more scrolling. I'm going to remove the `display: none` rule from `.svc-strip-desc` in the media query to make the descriptions visible.

I also need to adjust the padding on `services-inner` and the vertical spacing on `services-section` for smaller screens. I'm adding a media query at 768px to reduce the horizontal padding from 34px to 20px and trim the vertical padding from 100px/80px down to something more appropriate for mobile.

The Metrics section already handles the 480px breakpoint correctly by switching to a single column. Now I'm checking the Contact section to see what adjustments it needs at the top.

Reducing the contact section's padding...

Looking at the FeaturedWork component now to see if there are any horizontal scroll issues with the terminal card, especially since the text uses `white-space: pre-wrap` and might have long lines that could overflow on smaller screens.

I should also verify that all the animations work properly on mobile—scroll-triggered animations, parallax effects, marquee scrolling, and intersection observer reveals should all function fine, though I need to check if any `whileHover` effects from Framer Motion might cause problems on touch devices since hover states don't apply the same way. apply on first tap, which is a minor issue but acceptable. Now I'm mapping out the implementation plan: updating the NavBar with a hamburger menu and mobile overlay, adding the services section ID, adjusting CSS across Services, Projects, Process, About, and Contact components for mobile responsiveness, and reducing padding where needed. I should also check if `TextHoverHeading` is being used in the visible components.

Looking at the App.jsx imports, I can see that `TextHoverHeading` and `ContactShader` aren't actually imported, so those components don't need mobile fixes. Now I'm ready to start implementing the code changes.

First, I need to check what text content is in `FeaturedWork.jsx` to understand what I'm working with.</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Everything looks correct. Here's a summary of all the mobile responsive changes made to `src`:

**NavBar (hamburger menu)**
- Added a hamburger button (3-line → X animation) that shows at ≤900px
- Added a full-screen slide-in mobile menu with large nav links and "Book a call" CTA
- At ≤480px, the desktop "Book a call" button is hidden (menu has it)

**Services**
- Added `id="services"` so the nav `#services` anchor link actually works
- Removed the hiding of description text on mobile — services now show full desc on phone
- Section padding and inner padding reduced for mobile

**Projects**
- Disabled `position: sticky` on mobile (≤600px) with `top: 0 !important` so project cards stack naturally instead of overlapping
- Section padding reduced

**Process**
- Fixed hardcoded `padding: 0 34px` on `.process-head-inner` and `.process-log` — now uses 20px on mobile
- Row padding reduced, sticky header band tightened

**About**
- Fixed the character animation font — changed `clamp(46px, 9vw, 140px)` → `clamp(30px, 9vw, 140px)` so the big headline text fits on small screens without overflowing
- Stack section padding respects mobile gutters (20px sides instead of 34px)
- Word-line lead text font size reduced on small screens

**Contact**
- Reduced top padding from 118px → 70px on mobile

**Hero**
- Uses `100svh` on mobile (respects mobile browser chrome)
- Slightly reduced top padding and gaps on small screens

**Metrics**
- Reduced top/bottom padding on mobile, kept 2-column grid (doesn't collapse to 1 column which looked sparse)

---

_**User**_

"Who you'd work with" section is kinda broken on phone, animation completes too late so i don't get to read it on phone size, and its overall a bit buggy. laptop size performace is perfect so only the behaviour on phone needs to be changed

---

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

The problem is clear: the scroll-driven character/word/block animations in About are tied to `scrollYProgress` with ranges like `[0, 0.55]`, `[0.35, 0.78]` etc. On mobile the section is very tall, users scroll past it fast, and the text assembles right as it's leaving the screen — not as it's entering. The fix: detect touch devices and use instant `whileInView` animations instead of scroll-driven ones on mobile.

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Clean. Here's what was done and why:

**Root cause:** The About section uses `scrollYProgress` from `['start 98%', 'center 15%']` — meaning all animations are tied to how far you've scrolled relative to the section's full length. On a tall mobile section, the characters finish assembling right as the banner is leaving the screen. Users couldn't read it because readable = almost off-screen.

**Fix — mobile gets a completely separate render path:**

- `useFinePointer()` detects touch devices (returns `false` on phones — same hook already used to disable the cursor)
- On **mobile** (`!fine`): every element uses `whileInView` — as soon as the element enters the viewport it immediately animates in (simple fade + slide up). Content is fully readable the instant it appears.
  - `CharLineMobile` replaces scatter chars with a whole-line fade-in
  - `FadeBlock` replaces `SlideBlock` with a plain viewport-triggered fade
  - Capability cards stagger in with `whileInView`  
  - Stack chips use a simpler fade-scale instead of the wide ±280px scatter (which could cause layout jitter)
- On **desktop** (`fine`): absolutely nothing changed — same scroll-driven choreography as before

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

That black screen was a React crash, not a styling issue.

**What went wrong:** `About` used `if (!fine) return <Mobile />` vs `return <Desktop />` in the same component. `useFinePointer()` started as `false`, so the first render used the mobile path. On the next render (desktop/laptop), it switched to desktop and called many extra `useTransform` hooks — React threw *"Rendered more hooks than during the previous render"* and the whole app failed to render.

**What I fixed:**

1. **Split into two components** — `AboutMobile` and `AboutDesktop` are separate, each with a stable hook count. The parent just picks one: `fine ? <AboutDesktop /> : <AboutMobile />`.

2. **Correct initial pointer detection** — `useFinePointer()` now reads `(pointer: fine)` on first render instead of defaulting to `false`, so laptop/desktop gets the right layout immediately.

3. **Moved hooks out of loops/conditionals** — `WordItem`, `CapabilityCard`, `AboutLabel`, etc. are their own components so `useTransform` is never called inside `.map()` or conditional branches.

Refresh the page — the hero and nav should show on load again. Desktop scroll animations are unchanged; mobile still uses the simpler `whileInView` animations.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The mobile overlap comes from the timeline spine positioning formula. The text is too large for small screens. The sizing needs to scale down to match the phone feel without changing the laptop layout.

The spine overlaps because single-column layouts lack left padding. The title text is clamped to 32px on mobile. The section title is clamped to 22px.

App.css contains a `.section-title` class with a larger `clamp(38px, 6vw, 92px)` range. Because the process section title carries both classes, this global rule may override the smaller `.process-section-title` clamp. The import order between Process.css and App.css determines which declaration wins.

Process.css loads after App.css. The `.process-section-title` rule therefore overrides the global `.section-title` declaration. The title text is not being enlarged by the larger global clamp.

The process title is clamped to 32px on mobile, which feels too large. The watermark is clamped to 80px, so it likely overlaps the right-side text. On mobile, the left column stacks the stamp and node above the title and description, leaving no left margin for the timeline spine.

The spine sits near the left edge while the title and description stretch full-width across the center. Left padding on the content row or a dedicated timeline column would keep the text clear of the spine. Font sizes for the title, section heading, and watermark should also shrink on mobile.

The spine lands near 88 pixels on mobile while the title starts at 20 pixels, causing the overlap. Two fixes are under consideration: adding 32 pixels of left padding to the content row and pinning the spine to a fixed left offset, or indenting the title and description columns instead.

A simpler fix is preferred over a complex grid restructure. The plan is to add 28 pixels of left padding to the mobile content row and pin the spine to a fixed left offset of 20 pixels. The stamp and node will sit in the left gutter, while the title and description span the full width and begin after that gutter. This keeps the layout lightweight without restructuring the entire timeline.

The spine offset is refined to 29 pixels, combining the 20-pixel left padding with half the node width plus a small offset. The title clamp is tightened to 32 pixels on mobile. The JavaScript spine calculation should remain accurate even if the left column is narrowed. A cleaner mobile layout is now being drafted.

A two-column mobile layout was drafted with a 20-pixel left gutter and a 1fr right column. Using `display: contents` on the left wrapper was rejected because it would break the node reference needed for spine measurement. The plan is shifting back to a single-column layout with left margins added to the title and description instead.

The mobile layout is now a single column with 36 pixels of left padding to clear the spine. The left wrapper will be absolutely positioned at 20 pixels from the left edge, and the stamp will shrink to 8 pixels. This preserves the node references used for spine measurement while keeping the text clear of the timeline line.

The mobile layout is now fixed at 44 pixels of left padding and a 20-pixel absolute offset for the left column. The stamp shrinks to 8 pixels with a 48-pixel max width. The node wrapper and dot are both set to 14 pixels.

The 22-pixel minimum on mobile is too large for a 390-pixel screen. The title should scale proportionally rather than staying fixed at 22 pixels.</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Two clear things to fix:

1. **Project card `data-reveal` animation not firing on mobile** — the `useReveal` hook fires on scroll but sticky cards may have already been "measured" past the threshold. More directly: the cards use `data-reveal` which relies on `useReveal` from `App.jsx`. On mobile the sticky is removed but `data-reveal` is still there and should work. The real culprit is `[data-reveal][data-parallax].is-revealed { transform: none }` in App.css overriding the animation — but actually looking more carefully, the reveal hook uses IntersectionObserver and should work. The issue is the `data-project-card` cursor hover enlargement — irrelevant. The reveal animation uses `opacity: 0 + translateY(22px)` and fires via IntersectionObserver. It should work on mobile. Let me check if the `data-reveal` reveal is actually firing or if the cards are staying invisible.

Actually, re-reading `Projects.css` at ≤600px: `position: relative; top: 0 !important` — that's fine. The `data-reveal` on each card should still trigger via IntersectionObserver. The sticky behavior was removed, but the `[data-reveal]` transition timing is the same. The **real issue** is the project cards on mobile may not be triggering because `useReveal` checks `getBoundingClientRect().top < vh * 0.92` — and the `data-delay` on cards (`0`, `70`, `140`) means the staggered delay is inherited. This should still work.

Let me just check the sticky `top` style — on mobile we set `position: relative; top: 0 !important` in CSS, but the JSX still sets `style={{ top: \`${p.stickyTop}px\` }}` inline. Inline styles beat CSS, so on mobile the cards still have `position: relative` but with the inline `top` value, which pushes them down significantly. That's the bug — the reveal fires but the cards are displaced off-screen by the inline `top` style.

Fix: null out the `top` on mobile via a JS media query check, or simply never set the `top` style when it won't be sticky. The cleanest fix is to pass `top` only when sticky applies.

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

**What was done:**

**Project card reveal animation fix**
The root cause was subtle: inline `style={{ top: '144px' }}` etc. was still being applied on mobile even though CSS set `position: relative`. Since `top` works on `position: relative` elements too, every card was being offset down by its `stickyTop` value (96–288px) — pushing them way off screen so IntersectionObserver never triggered the reveal animation.

The fix: `Projects.jsx` now reads `useFinePointer()` and only applies the `top` style on desktop (`fine === true`). On mobile, no inline `top` is set at all so cards flow naturally and the `data-reveal` animation fires correctly as you scroll.

**Terminal block height reduction**
At ≤768px: smaller padding (`24px → 16px`), tighter `line-height` (`1.9 → 1.7`), smaller chrome/footer text and padding. At ≤480px: font drops to `9.5px`, line-height `1.65`, and even tighter padding. This shaves a significant amount of height without changing the desktop look at all.

---

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Clean. Here's the fix:

**What was happening:** `style={{ top: '96px' }}` (up to `288px`) was still applied on every card as an inline style. CSS `position: relative; top: 0 !important` from the previous attempt was meant to override it, but `position: sticky` with a large `top` value is still sticky — it just sits 96–288px below the viewport top before it locks. Cards further down the list (top: 288px) were appearing to not animate because they were offset so far down they were scrolling off-screen while "stuck".

**The fix:** On mobile (≤600px), a CSS `top: 56px` override takes precedence over the inline style (CSS specificity rules apply — but actually inline styles beat class styles). 

Actually, `top: 56px` in a media query **will not** override an inline style. Let me fix this properly:

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="edit_file_v2">

<details><summary>Tool use: **edit_file_v2** • Edit file: d:\Aditya\Programming\portfolio\src\components\styles\Projects.css</summary>

</details>

</tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="edit_file_v2"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **edit_file_v2**</summary>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

The `!important` is necessary here because inline styles have the highest specificity — without it the CSS media query rule would lose to `style={{ top: '288px' }}`. Now on phone all 9 cards stick at 56px from the top and stack on top of each other as you scroll, same visual feel as desktop just with tighter offsets that fit the smaller screen.

A clamp around 62px–78px at 18–20vw would scale the text larger on mobile. The exact visual height depends on the SVG viewBox ratio, so the value may need tuning.

The text currently occupies a large share of the 210px viewBox height. A clamp of 60px–82px at 19vw would yield roughly 74px on a 390px phone, which still feels too small. The user wants it to feel as prominent as the laptop heading. The laptop heading uses a 38px–92px clamp instead.

A clamp near 70px–86px at 26vw would yield roughly 86px on a 375px phone, nearly matching the desktop 82px. Two lines might overflow on very small screens. The previous 52px/14vw clamp was too low.

A clamp of 72px–22vw–82px is being considered for the mobile breakpoint. Removing the cap entirely and using a 72px minimum is another option. The SVG viewBox may need adjustment to prevent clipping when the font grows larger.

The chosen clamp is 76px–24vw–82px. This yields roughly 76px on a 320px phone and caps at 82px on larger mobile screens. The existing 640px breakpoint will be used directly.

The clamp is being raised to 78px–25vw–82px. Section titles elsewhere use a 38px minimum, so the heading is already much larger than surrounding text. The perceived smallness may instead come from the SVG viewBox scaling, letter-stretching, or the stroke-only style lacking a fill.

Setting the mobile font size to a flat 82px would match the desktop heading exactly in SVG units. The SVG scales to its container width, so the text would remain proportionally sized on both devices. The inner container width shrinks from roughly 1250px on desktop to about 350px on mobile, which affects how the text fills the space.

The exact visual height of the text is being calculated. An 82px font inside a 700px-wide viewBox represents roughly 11.7% of the SVG width. On a 350px phone screen, that yields approximately 41px of visual character height. The precise mapping between CSS pixel units and SVG viewBox coordinates is being verified.

An 88px font risks clipping the second line inside the 210px viewBox. The user confirmed the previous clamp was still too small. A flat 82px on mobile is being reconsidered, or a tighter clamp such as 80px–28vw–82px.

The chosen fix is a flat 82px font size on mobile, matching the desktop heading exactly. Scaling the SVG wrapper with a CSS transform was rejected as too hacky. If the text still feels too small, the viewBox height could be tightened or the font size increased further.

A 92px font size with adjusted y coordinates is being considered to make the heading visually larger on mobile. This would require a JSX change rather than a CSS-only fix. A CSS clamp of 84px–28vw–96px is also being weighed, though it risks clipping the text.</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

The problem was a mobile CSS rule shrinking the heading to 52–72px while desktop uses 82px — so it looked small relative to everything else on phone.

**Changes:**
- **Removed** the mobile font shrink entirely
- On touch devices, font size is now **96px** (vs 82px on laptop) so it reads with similar visual weight on a narrow screen
- SVG **viewBox and line positions** are adjusted on mobile so the larger text doesn’t clip

Laptop stays at 82px with the same layout. Refresh on your phone and the heading should feel much closer in prominence to the rest of the section.
